questions.text: I find that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that most consistently drives me to reconsider whether I am doing enough.
I notice that major life events produce a proportionate, not outsized, emotional response in me.
I notice when two people in a group have unspoken friction between them.
I find that I can move through emotionally difficult conversations without carrying the weight of them afterward.
My body seems to amplify emotional signals — what starts as mild annoyance quickly becomes agitation.
I often position myself as the final decision-maker in group settings.
I can tell when someone is smiling politely but is actually upset.
I feel that a well-structured routine is essential to productivity.
I feel most alive when I'm improvising my way through an unstructured situation.
I feel that groups benefit from working through inefficiency at their own pace.
I feel uneasy when I don't have a clear plan for the week ahead.
I tend to put my career milestones at the center of my life decisions.
I tend to miss when someone is trying to end a conversation politely.
I routinely give up my free time so that others can get what they need.
I experience the passage of time differently when I'm emotionally activated — minutes can feel like hours.
I feel compelled to act when I see inefficiency or lack of direction.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the principle that should guide not only individual conduct but also the design of social systems.
I hold the view that generosity is not about what you have but about how much you're willing to let go of.
